title: Pension Schemes Act 1993
chapters: c.48. 195 sections + 9 Schedules in 13 Parts. The foundational Act governing occupational and personal pension schemes in the UK. Must be read alongside the Pensions Act 1995 (scheme governance, minimum funding), Pensions Act 2004 (Pension Regulator, Pension Protection Fund, scheme funding), and Pensions Act 2008 (automatic enrolment). Together, these four Acts form the UK pensions legislative framework. The 1993 Act covers: the registration and structure of pension schemes, contracting-out of the state scheme (now abolished from April 2016), preservation of benefit rights for early leavers, cash equivalent transfer values, and disclosure of information to members.

parliamentaryOrigin.introducedBy: Government Bill — Conservative Government. The 1993 Act was a consolidation Act, bringing together earlier pensions legislation including the Social Security Pensions Act 1975 and the Social Security Act 1986.
parliamentaryOrigin.commencementNotes: Consolidation Act — brought together existing pensions legislation into a single framework. Has been substantially amended by the Pensions Act 1995, Pensions Act 2004, Pensions Act 2008, and Pension Schemes Act 2015. Despite these amendments, the 1993 Act remains the foundational statute — it defines what a pension scheme IS and establishes the core rights of scheme members.

significantAmendments.effect: Introduced the Occupational Pensions Regulatory Authority (OPRA — later replaced by the Pensions Regulator). Imposed minimum funding requirements on defined benefit schemes. Strengthened member protections. Introduced pension sharing on divorce.

Established the Pensions Regulator (TPR) — replaced OPRA. Created the Pension Protection Fund (PPF) — protects members of DB schemes when the employer becomes insolvent and the scheme is underfunded. Introduced scheme-specific funding requirements. Gave TPR powers to issue contribution notices and financial support directions.

Introduced AUTOMATIC ENROLMENT — the duty on employers to automatically enrol eligible jobholders into a qualifying workplace pension scheme. Minimum contribution: 8% of qualifying earnings (3% employer, 5% employee from April 2019). Created NEST (National Employment Savings Trust) as a default scheme. This is the Act that transformed UK workplace pensions from voluntary to quasi-compulsory.

Abolished contracting-out for defined benefit schemes from 6 April 2016. Introduced the framework for collective defined contribution (CDC) pension schemes. Introduced pension freedoms (from April 2015 — accessed from age 55, individuals can draw their entire DC pot as cash, subject to marginal rate income tax on amounts above the 25% tax-free lump sum).

precedingDocuments.title: The Four-Act UK Pensions Framework
precedingDocuments.significance: The UK pensions framework is built on four principal Acts that must be read together. PSA 1993: defines pension schemes, member rights, preservation, transfers. PA 1995: governance, funding, regulatory body. PA 2004: Pensions Regulator, PPF, enhanced scheme funding. PA 2008: automatic enrolment. No single Act tells the whole story — Eileen must draw from all four.

precedingDocuments.title: The Pensions Regulator (TPR)
precedingDocuments.significance: TPR is the statutory regulator of workplace pension schemes. It enforces auto-enrolment compliance, monitors DB scheme funding, and has powers to fine employers who fail to comply with their pension duties. TPR can issue fixed penalty notices (£400) and escalating penalty notices (up to £50,000 per day for large employers) for auto-enrolment non-compliance.

leadingCases.name: Limited higher court employment case law — pensions disputes typically handled by the Pensions Ombudsman or specialist Pensions Court
leadingCases.principle: Pension disputes between members and schemes are typically resolved by the Pensions Ombudsman (free, independent dispute resolution service). Employment tribunal jurisdiction over pension disputes is limited — the ET can deal with auto-enrolment detriment claims (ERA 1996 s.47AA) and unfair dismissal for asserting pension rights (ERA 1996 s.104D), but substantive pension scheme disputes go to the Ombudsman or the court.
leadingCases.significance: The HR manager must know where pension disputes are resolved: auto-enrolment employment issues → employment tribunal; pension scheme benefit disputes → Pensions Ombudsman; pension scheme governance/funding → Pensions Regulator.

parts.title: Part 1 — Employer Obligations: Auto-Enrolment, Contributions, and Compliance

sections.title: What every employer must do — auto-enrolment, minimum contributions, and the Pensions Regulator's enforcement powers
sections.summary: Since the Pensions Act 2008 phased in automatic enrolment (2012-2018), EVERY UK employer — regardless of size — must automatically enrol eligible employees into a qualifying workplace pension scheme and make minimum contributions. The PSA 1993 defines what constitutes a pension scheme and the quality standards it must meet; the PA 2008 creates the auto-enrolment duty. An 'eligible jobholder' is a worker aged between 22 and State Pension age who earns above the earnings trigger (£10,000/year from April 2024). The employer must contribute at least 3% of qualifying earnings; the total minimum contribution (employer + employee) is 8%. The employer must also re-enrol eligible employees who have opted out approximately every 3 years. The Pensions Regulator (TPR) enforces compliance with significant penalty powers.
sections.text:
AUTO-ENROLMENT — EMPLOYER DUTIES:

(1) AUTOMATICALLY ENROL eligible jobholders into a qualifying workplace pension scheme within 6 weeks of their start date (or the date they become eligible).
(2) An ELIGIBLE JOBHOLDER is: aged 22 to State Pension age, earning above the earnings trigger (£10,000/year from April 2024), working (or ordinarily working) in the UK.
(3) NON-ELIGIBLE JOBHOLDERS (aged 16-21 or SPA-74, earning above £6,240 but below £10,000) have the right to OPT IN — the employer must enrol them if they request it and must make the minimum employer contribution.
(4) ENTITLED WORKERS (earning below £6,240) have the right to JOIN a pension scheme but the employer does not have to contribute.

MINIMUM CONTRIBUTIONS (from April 2019):
— Employer minimum: 3% of qualifying earnings
— Employee minimum: 5% of qualifying earnings
— Total minimum: 8% of qualifying earnings
— 'Qualifying earnings' means earnings between the lower and upper limits (£6,240 to £50,270 from April 2024).
— The employer may choose to contribute more than the minimum.

OPTING OUT:
(1) An employee has the right to opt out within ONE MONTH of being enrolled.
(2) If they opt out within the one-month window, ALL contributions (employer and employee) must be refunded.
(3) An employee who opts out must be automatically RE-ENROLLED approximately every 3 years (on the re-enrolment date).
(4) The employer must NOT encourage or induce employees to opt out — this is a criminal offence.

COMPLIANCE AND ENFORCEMENT (TPR):
(1) The employer must complete a DECLARATION OF COMPLIANCE with TPR within 5 months of their duties start date.
(2) The employer must keep RECORDS of auto-enrolment for 6 years (or 4 years for opt-out notices).
(3) TPR can issue FIXED PENALTY NOTICES (£400) for non-compliance.
(4) TPR can issue ESCALATING PENALTY NOTICES: £50/day (1-4 staff), £500/day (5-49 staff), £2,500/day (50-249 staff), £10,000/day (250-499 staff), up to £50,000/day (500+ staff).
(5) Persistent non-compliance can lead to CRIMINAL PROSECUTION.

PROTECTION FOR EMPLOYEES:
(1) An employer must NOT dismiss or subject an employee to detriment for:
(a) Being enrolled in a pension scheme.
(b) Opting in to a pension scheme.
(c) Refusing to opt out.
(d) Making a complaint about pension arrangements.
(2) Dismissal for a pension-related reason is AUTOMATICALLY UNFAIR (ERA 1996 s.104D — no qualifying period).
(3) Detriment for a pension-related reason is actionable (ERA 1996 s.47AA).
sections.keyPrinciple: Auto-enrolment is not optional. Every employer — from a sole trader with one employee to a multinational corporation — must comply. The employer cannot avoid the duty by not choosing a scheme (NEST is available as a default). The employer cannot avoid the duty by persuading employees to opt out (this is a criminal offence). The HR manager must ensure: a qualifying scheme is in place, eligible employees are enrolled on time, contributions are paid on time, opt-outs are processed correctly, re-enrolment happens every 3 years, and records are maintained.
refs: PSA 1993 s.1 (definition of occupational pension scheme)
Automatic Enrolment (Earnings Trigger and Qualifying Earnings Band) Order (annual thresholds)
ERA 1996 s.104D (automatic unfair dismissal for pension-related reason)
NEST (National Employment Savings Trust) — default auto-enrolment scheme

regulatoryGuidance.title: Automatic enrolment detailed guidance for employers
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Comprehensive guidance on auto-enrolment duties, including step-by-step compliance guides, contribution calculations, and record-keeping requirements.

commonErrors: Assuming auto-enrolment only applies to large employers — it applies to ALL employers, regardless of size. A business with one employee must comply
Not enrolling eligible employees within 6 weeks of their start date — late enrolment means backdated contributions must be paid. TPR can impose penalties for persistent late enrolment
Encouraging employees to opt out — inducement to opt out is a CRIMINAL OFFENCE. The employer must present opt-out as a neutral choice, not encourage or pressure it
Not re-enrolling employees who previously opted out — approximately every 3 years, the employer must re-enrol eligible employees who opted out. This is frequently forgotten

Treating all workers as 'self-employed' to avoid auto-enrolment — worker status is determined by the reality of the relationship, not the label. Workers who meet the eligible jobholder criteria must be enrolled regardless of what the contract calls them

Not paying contributions on time — employer contributions must be paid to the scheme by the 22nd of the month following deduction (or the 19th for cheque payments). Late payment is a breach that TPR can penalise
Dismissing or penalising an employee for refusing to opt out — this is automatically unfair dismissal (ERA 1996 s.104D) with no qualifying period

parts.title: Part 2 — Employee Rights: Understanding Your Pension

sections.num: PSA 1993 Parts IV-VI + PA 2015 pension freedoms
sections.title: What every employee needs to understand — preservation, transfers, pension freedoms, and what happens when you leave your job
sections.summary: The PSA 1993 establishes the core rights that protect pension scheme members, particularly those who leave their employment before retirement ('early leavers'). These rights include: the right to have pension benefits PRESERVED when leaving employment (they do not disappear), the right to a CASH EQUIVALENT TRANSFER VALUE (CETV) to move benefits to another scheme, the right to INFORMATION about the scheme and their benefits, and protection against DISCRIMINATION. Since April 2015, the pension freedoms (Pension Schemes Act 2015) have given individuals aged 55+ the right to access their entire defined contribution pension pot, either as a lump sum or through drawdown, subject to income tax on amounts above the 25% tax-free lump sum.
sections.text:
PRESERVATION OF BENEFITS — EARLY LEAVERS (PSA 1993 Part IV, ss.69-101):

(1) If you leave your job, your accrued pension benefits are PRESERVED — they do not disappear.
(2) For defined benefit (DB) schemes: your preserved pension is a 'deferred pension' based on your years of service and (usually) your salary at leaving. It must be revalued (increased) each year in line with inflation (up to a cap) so it maintains its real value.
(3) For defined contribution (DC) schemes: your pension pot remains invested. The fund belongs to YOU, not your employer. It continues to grow (or shrink) based on investment performance.
(4) You CANNOT be required to forfeit your pension when you leave — with very limited exceptions (e.g., less than 30 days' scheme membership, or a criminal offence against the employer).

CASH EQUIVALENT TRANSFER VALUES — CETVs (PSA 1993 Part IV Ch.IV, ss.93-101):

(1) When you leave employment, you have the right to a STATEMENT of the cash equivalent transfer value of your pension benefits.
(2) You can use this CETV to TRANSFER your pension to: another employer's scheme, a personal pension, a SIPP (Self-Invested Personal Pension), or an overseas pension scheme (qualifying recognised overseas pension scheme — QROPS).
(3) The CETV must be calculated using actuarial methods that represent the value of the benefits being given up.
(4) You should take INDEPENDENT FINANCIAL ADVICE before transferring, especially from a DB scheme — once transferred, you lose the guaranteed income promise of the DB scheme.
(5) Since April 2015: if the CETV exceeds £30,000, you are LEGALLY REQUIRED to take independent financial advice before transferring from a DB scheme (Pension Schemes Act 2015 s.48).

PENSION FREEDOMS (PA 2015 — from April 2015):

(1) From age 55 (rising to 57 from April 2028), individuals can access their entire DC pension pot.
(2) Options: take the entire pot as a lump sum, use drawdown (take flexible amounts over time), buy an annuity (guaranteed income for life), or a combination.
(3) 25% of the pot can be taken TAX-FREE. The remaining 75% is subject to income tax at the individual's marginal rate.
(4) CAUTION: taking a large lump sum can push the individual into a higher tax band for that year.
(5) Pension freedoms do NOT apply to DB scheme benefits while they remain in the DB scheme — but if the member transfers out to a DC scheme, the freedoms then apply to the transferred amount.

INFORMATION AND DISCLOSURE (PSA 1993 Part VI + Disclosure Regulations):

(1) Scheme members have the right to: an annual benefit statement, scheme accounts, the scheme's statement of investment principles, the scheme's funding position (DB schemes), and information about changes to the scheme.
(2) Members can request information from trustees at any time.
(3) The employer must provide a PENSION CONTRIBUTION NOTICE on each payslip showing the employer and employee contributions.

WHAT HAPPENS TO YOUR PENSION WHEN YOU LEAVE YOUR JOB:
(a) DC scheme: your pot stays with the pension provider. You can leave it there, transfer it, or (from age 55) access it.
(b) DB scheme: your benefits are preserved as a deferred pension. You can leave them, transfer them (take advice first), or claim them at the scheme's normal retirement age.
(c) If your employer becomes INSOLVENT: DC pots are SAFE (held by the provider, not the employer). DB schemes may be protected by the PPF if underfunded.
(d) If you are made REDUNDANT: your pension rights are not affected by the redundancy. Your preserved benefits, CETV right, and transfer rights all continue.
sections.keyPrinciple: Your pension is YOUR money. It does not belong to your employer. When you leave a job, your pension benefits are preserved — they do not disappear. You have the right to information, the right to transfer, and (from age 55) the right to access your DC pot. The PSA 1993 ensures these rights exist; the later Acts strengthen them. Eileen's role is to ensure that every employee understands: (a) their pension does not vanish when they leave, (b) they have choices about what to do with it, (c) they should take independent financial advice before making major pension decisions, and (d) their employer cannot penalise them for exercising their pension rights.
refs: PSA 1993 Part IV (preservation of benefit rights — early leavers)
PSA 1993 Part VI (disclosure of information to members)
PA 2015 s.48 (mandatory advice for DB transfers above £30,000)
PA 2004 (Pension Protection Fund — DB scheme protection on employer insolvency)

regulatoryGuidance.title: Pensions guidance service — free, impartial pension guidance
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Free, Government-backed service providing impartial pension guidance. All individuals aged 50+ have the right to free Pension Wise guidance sessions (telephone, online, or face-to-face) before accessing their pension.

commonErrors: Assuming your pension disappears when you leave a job — it does NOT. Benefits are preserved. DC pots remain invested. DB benefits are held as deferred pensions with inflation protection

Transferring out of a DB scheme without taking independent financial advice — this is one of the most consequential financial decisions an employee can make. Since April 2015, advice is LEGALLY REQUIRED for DB transfers above £30,000. Without advice, the employee risks giving up a guaranteed lifetime income for an uncertain investment pot

Not knowing you have a pension — some employees, particularly in lower-paid or part-time work, may not realise they have been auto-enrolled. Check payslips for pension contributions. Contact HR or the pension provider for a benefit statement

Taking the entire DC pot as a lump sum without considering the tax consequences — only 25% is tax-free. The remaining 75% is subject to income tax. A large lump sum withdrawal can push the individual into the 40% or 45% tax band for that year

Assuming that employer insolvency means the pension is lost — DC pots are held by the pension provider, NOT the employer. They are SAFE. DB schemes are protected by the PPF if the scheme is underfunded and the employer is insolvent. Cross-reference: insolvency1986.json Part 1

Not checking old pensions from previous employment — many people have 'lost' pensions from previous employers. The Pension Tracing Service (GOV.UK) can help find them. These pensions have real value and should be located

Not accessing the free Pension Wise guidance — everyone aged 50+ has the right to free, impartial pension guidance from Pension Wise (part of MoneyHelper). This is Government-funded and should be used before making any pension decisions

pendingAmendments.instrument: Auto-enrolment expansion — lowering the age to 18 and removing the lower earnings limit
pendingAmendments.status: The Pensions (Extension of Automatic Enrolment) Act 2023 received Royal Assent in September 2023. It gives the Secretary of State powers to: (a) lower the auto-enrolment age from 22 to 18, and (b) remove the lower qualifying earnings limit (£6,240) so contributions are calculated from the first pound earned. Implementation dates have NOT yet been announced. Monitor for commencement orders.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: HIGH — when implemented, this will significantly increase the number of employees auto-enrolled and the employer's contribution costs

pendingAmendments.instrument: Normal minimum pension age increase from 55 to 57
pendingAmendments.status: From 6 April 2028, the normal minimum pension age increases from 55 to 57. Individuals who want to access their pension between 55 and 57 must do so before April 2028. Some schemes have a protected pension age retaining the right to access at 55.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: MODERATE — affects the timing of pension access for employees approaching retirement

pendingAmendments.status: The earnings trigger (£10,000) and qualifying earnings band (£6,240-£50,270) are reviewed annually. Monitor for April 2025/2026 updates.
